data.title: Standex: The Electronics Company the Market Still Reads as an Industrial
data.description:
What the Market Has Not Updated

For most of its history, Standex International was a reliable but unremarkable multi-industry manufacturer, the kind of business that analysts filed under diversified industrials and revisited occasionally, usually to note that margins were acceptable and end-market exposure was broad. That characterization was accurate enough for a company selling engraving tools, food service equipment, hydraulic cylinders, and reed switches to whoever needed them. It is no longer accurate, and the gap between what the business has become and how the market continues to price it is the animating question for anyone evaluating the stock today.

The transformation is not a story management is telling. It is visible in the filings. Electronics, the segment built on reed switches and magnetic sensors that once sat comfortably alongside Engraving and Specialty Solutions as one of five roughly equivalent revenue contributors, now represents 52% of sales and 63% of adjusted operating income. The adjustment did not happen through organic drift. It happened through a combination of deliberate portfolio pruning, years of exiting lower-margin, lower-growth businesses, and the October 2024 acquisition of Amran Instrument Transformers and Narayan Powertech, a $462 million transaction that was the largest in the company's history and that doubled its exposure to what management calls fast-growth markets: electrical grid modernization, defense electronics, space commercialization, and electric vehicles.

These are not incidental markets. Electrical grid modernization is a multi-decade spending cycle driven by three converging forces: aging infrastructure across developed economies, rising demand from data centers supporting AI infrastructure, and the need for transmission capacity to support renewable energy integration. Standex, through the Amran and Narayan businesses, is a custom-component supplier to the major OEMs in that market, Eaton, GE, Schneider Electric, providing the instrument transformers that enable accurate measurement, protection, and control of grid networks. This is not a commodity position. The products are engineered specifically for individual customer applications, specified by engineers, and unlikely to be switched after qualification. The relationship model is closer to an embedded partner than a vendor.

The stock has re-rated materially over the past twelve months, and the direction of that movement is correct. A business whose dominant segment was growing at double-digit organic rates and earning adjusted operating margins approaching 20% at the company level should not have been priced where it was. The re-rating acknowledges that.

What it does not yet reflect is a more precise question: what should the Electronics segment be worth on its own terms? Backed out of the company-level enterprise value, the implied EBITDA multiple being applied to a business generating adjusted operating margins above 28% on grid, defense, and space components sits near 20 to 22 times. AMETEK earns lower margins, grows more slowly, and trades at 22 times. Curtiss-Wright's defense and power segments trade at 33 times. The premium attached to those businesses for owning exactly the same economics, custom-engineered components, long qualification cycles, specification-driven customer relationships,has not found its way into the implied valuation of this segment yet.

It has not.

Anatomy of Resilience

Standex operates across five reporting segments, but the investment story concentrates in one of them with unusual clarity. The other four segments, Engraving, Engineering Technologies, Scientific, and Specialty Solutions, represent roughly half of revenue but collectively generate less than 40% of adjusted operating income.

Engraving, contributing approximately 16% of sales, provides mold texturizing and tooling for automotive and industrial customers. It is a slow-growth cash generator whose organic revenue growth is constrained by the pace of new automotive platform launches. Engineering Technologies, at roughly 14% of sales, produces near-net formed components for aerospace and defense applications under the Spincraft and McStarlite brands, with revenue tied directly to Boeing and Airbus production cycles. Scientific, a high-margin niche business generating adjusted operating margins above 24%, ran into a real but temporary headwind as NIH funding cuts deferred purchasing from research institutions. Specialty Solutions, the smallest segment at roughly 9% of sales, is the one management has most clearly identified as non-core; the divestiture of Federal Industries in early 2026 is the clearest signal of where this segment is heading.

Electronics is built around three product families. Switches and sensors, principally reed switches and reed relays, serve HVAC, industrial automation, and security applications, growing at mid-to-upper single-digit organic rates and generating steady cash flow. Magnetics, covering wound transformers, inductors, and power conversion components, serves power supply and energy conversion applications. The third and most consequential family is the Grid business, centered on the Amran and Narayan platform, which manufactures current and voltage instrument transformers for low and medium voltage electrical grid equipment. Grid business sales have grown roughly 35% since the acquisition closed, and management's long-term target of over $340 million in fast-growth market sales by fiscal 2028 is predominantly this story.

The business Amran and Narayan brought is structurally attractive in ways that are not immediately obvious from the product description. Instrument transformers are the measuring and sensing elements inside switchgear and protection systems; they tell grid operators exactly what is happening in a circuit at any moment, enabling protection relays to respond correctly when something goes wrong. Amran and Narayan entered the Standex portfolio with an adjusted EBITDA margin above 40%, against a company-wide average in the high teens. The products are custom-engineered, the qualification processes are long, and the switching costs for grid OEMs are high. A utility commissioning a substation in 2026 is specifying components that will remain in service for twenty to thirty years. The choice of instrument transformer supplier is not revisited casually.

What makes the comparison with ITT, AMETEK, and Curtiss-Wright illuminating rather than merely convenient is that all three are businesses whose dominant revenue streams come from engineered components sold into long-cycle, specification-driven markets. The economics of customer intimacy, long replacement cycles, and high switching costs are not unique to Standex. But Standex's Electronics segment is currently generating those economics at adjusted operating margins above 28%, comparable to or better than what AMETEK and ITT report for their most premium segments, while the company-level multiple still reflects the lower-margin businesses sitting alongside it in the portfolio.

The more pointed question is why a reader drawn to the electrical grid modernization theme would select a component supplier over the system integrators, ABB, Eaton, Schneider Electric, that sell the complete switchgear and protection systems into which Standex's instrument transformers are installed. The answer begins with an observation: those companies are not alternatives to this position. They are the customers. Standex does not compete with ABB for a grid contract. It supplies ABB with the current and voltage measurement elements that enable ABB's own protection systems to function correctly. That positions the Grid business at the most defensible layer of the value chain, the custom-engineered, application-specific component that a system integrator cannot profitably internalize, without the commodity automation and lower-margin industrial work that sit alongside ABB's and Eaton's high-value segments. For an investor seeking concentrated exposure to grid modernization at the component economics rather than the system level, ABB and Eaton represent the market being addressed. Standex is the pick-and-shovel inside it.

The Inflection in the Numbers

The financial trajectory of the past eighteen months tells a story that the company's headline earnings multiple has not yet absorbed. In fiscal 2022, Standex generated $663 million in revenue at an adjusted operating margin of 15.4%. In the trailing twelve months ended December 31, 2025, the same company generated $868.5 million in revenue at an adjusted operating margin approaching 20%, with the Electronics segment alone running above 28%. That is not incremental improvement. It is a fundamentally different earnings profile attached to a multiple that has not fully repriced for it.

The most recent earnings release, covering the second fiscal quarter of 2026 ended December 31, 2025, crystallized the inflection. Total revenue reached $221.3 million, a 16.6% increase year-on-year, with 6.4% of that coming from organic sources. The Electronics segment posted $115.7 million in revenue, up 20.6% year-on-year, with organic growth of 11.1% and a book-to-bill ratio of 1.08, its sixth consecutive quarter at or near 1.0 or above. Adjusted operating margin for the company reached 19.0%, up 30 basis points year-on-year. Free cash flow was $13.0 million in the quarter, compared to $2.2 million in the same period a year earlier.

The new product engine deserves direct attention because it is the mechanism through which organic growth compounds independently of acquisition-driven scale. Standex released 16 new products in fiscal 2025, generating $55 million in new product sales against $38 million the prior year. In fiscal 2026, the company is targeting more than 15 additional launches expected to contribute approximately 300 basis points of incremental revenue growth. This is not a normal industrial product development cycle, it is a structured, measured program that management tracks quarterly and that is producing compounding results. New product sales in the second fiscal quarter of 2026 grew 13% year-on-year and contributed 7% to total revenue.

Fast-growth market sales totaled approximately $61 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 and represented 28% of total company revenue. For fiscal year 2026, management has reaffirmed guidance for fast-growth market sales to exceed $270 million, a greater than 45% increase year-on-year. More than half of that figure is attributable to grid solutions, with the remainder distributed across defense, space, renewable energy, and electric vehicles. Each of these sub-markets is structurally independent of the others, which means the revenue base has a degree of diversification within the fast-growth category that is not always fully appreciated from the outside.

The leverage trajectory is the remaining variable for an owner focused on balance sheet normalization. Net debt stood at $437.7 million at December 31, 2025, representing a ratio of 2.3 times adjusted EBITDA on the most recent trailing basis. Standex paid down approximately $10 million of debt during the second fiscal quarter and is committed to further reduction through fiscal 2026, with management guidance for interest expense of $7 to $7.5 million in the third quarter. Capital expenditures are guided at $33 to $38 million for the full fiscal year, reflecting deliberate investment in capacity expansion, a Croatia greenfield facility, additional manufacturing in Mexico and Houston, to support the Grid growth trajectory. These are not defensive expenditures. They are pre-investment in a backlog that management is already contracted to serve.

The five-year average ROIC of approximately 12.8% is the anchor that puts the current acquisition-suppressed figure of 6.5% in context. That historical ROIC reflects what the business earns on capital when the balance sheet is not carrying a large, recently closed transaction. The acquisition of Amran and Narayan was immediately accretive to revenue, margins, and earnings per share, as disclosed at announcement, but it also added over $460 million to the capital base. As debt is repaid and the acquired business continues to grow at the EBITDA margins above 40% that it brought in, the return on that capital will improve faster than the headline ROIC figure currently suggests.

Capital Allocation and the Portfolio Signal

Few things communicate a management team's view of their own business more precisely than where they choose to deploy capital and what they decide to exit. Over the past four years, Standex has been consistent on both fronts. The acquisitions have targeted businesses with above-average EBITDA margins, exposure to secular growth end markets, and engineering differentiation that provides a basis for customer stickiness. The divestitures have targeted businesses at the opposite end of that spectrum.

The $462 million acquisition of Amran and Narayan in October 2024 was the capstone of a portfolio transformation that began well before that transaction. McStarlite, acquired in fiscal 2025, added aerospace structural components to Engineering Technologies and exposed the segment to commercial aviation production ramps with longer visibility than the general industrial markets it had historically served. Custom Biogenic Systems extended Scientific's reach in cryogenic storage with a product line carrying margins consistent with the segment's existing premium positioning. Sanyu Switch, acquired in fiscal 2024, added reed switch manufacturing in Japan, deepening the Electronics segment's geographic presence in a market where customer proximity matters for specification work.

Each transaction was small relative to the Amran and Narayan deal, but each followed the same logic: add businesses with engineering differentiation, above-average margins, and exposure to end markets where replacement cycles are long and the competitive entry barriers are real. The divestiture of Federal Industries in early 2026, a refrigerated display case business in the Specialty Solutions segment, confirms that the same logic runs in reverse. Management does not appear to be harvesting Federal Industries' cash flow, they are simplifying the portfolio to concentrate management attention and capital on the segments where the growth opportunity is most compelling.

Capital returns sit alongside growth investment in the hierarchy, not above it. The 246th consecutive quarterly dividend was declared in January 2026 at $0.34 per share, a 6.3% increase year-on-year and a continuation of an unbroken dividend payment record stretching back to the company's public listing in November 1964. The five-year dividend growth rate of approximately 7.6% suggests management views the current earnings trajectory as sufficiently durable to commit to progressive distributions even while carrying meaningful leverage from the Amran and Narayan transaction. The $76 million share repurchase program completed in the fiscal second quarter was additive to capital return but reflects opportunistic execution rather than a primary allocation priority at this leverage level.

The capacity investment currently underway is the most important forward-looking signal in the capital allocation picture. The new greenfield facility in Croatia serves European grid customers and is expected to reach $60 million in sales over three to five years, based on existing customer commitments. Additional manufacturing capacity in Mexico and Houston is being added specifically to support Grid demand. These are not speculative investments. They are responding to contracted customer requirements, which means the revenue they will eventually produce is partially visible already in the backlog and in the order activity that drove a book-to-bill above 1.0 for six consecutive quarters in Electronics.

Who Owns This and What It Signals

Barrow Hanley Mewhinney and Strauss holds the largest visible institutional stake, 393,000 shares representing 0.28% of its portfolio, with a value of approximately $85.4 million as of December 31, 2025. The average buy price of $138.80 reflects accumulation at levels well below where the stock trades today. For a fund of Barrow Hanley's orientation, quality-oriented, long-duration, value-disciplined, a holding of this size and vintage is not a passive outcome. It represents a view, held and renewed over multiple years, that the earnings power of this business was consistently underappreciated relative to the price being asked.

Driehaus Capital Management, with 86,600 shares and an average acquisition price of $195.71, tells a different story. Where Barrow Hanley built its position from a fraction of today's prices, Driehaus increased its holding by 61%, approximately 32,800 shares, at levels much closer to the current range. Driehaus is a growth-oriented manager with a documented history of positioning in businesses where earnings acceleration is underway but not yet fully reflected in valuations. Fresh capital added at prices well above the historical cost basis of longer-tenured holders suggests an updated assessment of the current trajectory, not a carried position from an earlier view.

Jane Street's 317% position increase, approximately 34,500 additional shares to bring the total to 45,300, at an average price of $204.69 is the most striking activity in the register. Jane Street is not a fundamental research shop; it is a market-making and arbitrage firm that builds positions where it has formed specific views on pricing inefficiencies. An increase of that magnitude at prices near the top of the historical range signals a specific and recent judgment about relative value. The timing coincides with the period when the Amran and Narayan acquisition's contribution to earnings first became clearly visible in quarterly filings.

Gamco Investors, with 75,100 shares at an average buy price of $60.26, represents the long-duration capital that has watched this transformation from the beginning. A modest 3.6% trim does not change the message: an investor who understood what this business was becoming early enough to buy at $60 saw something the market at large has taken considerably longer to recognize.

The collective picture is a shareholder base rotating from patient accumulation to active conviction. The old money built at low prices is largely still present and largely untrimmed. Fresh capital is arriving at higher prices from managers specifically pricing growth acceleration. That rotation is not noise. It is the institutional analogue of the organic growth inflection visible in the quarterly filings.

What the Multiple Is Not Yet Pricing

At roughly $250 per share and approximately 12.1 million shares outstanding, Standex carries a market capitalization of approximately $3.0 billion. Net debt of $437.7 million as of December 31, 2025 brings the enterprise value to approximately $3.45 billion. Against trailing twelve-month adjusted EBITDA of approximately $191 million, that implies an EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple of approximately 18 times. Against free cash flow, still compressed by the elevated capital expenditure program currently underway, the implied multiple sits near 50 times on a trailing basis, a figure that reflects investment in growth infrastructure rather than normalized earnings conversion.

The more useful metric for this specific business at this specific moment is the forward EV/EBITDA multiple once the capacity expansion normalizes. Management has guided to fiscal year 2026 revenue exceeding $900 million, with continued adjusted operating margin expansion. Using a conservative assumption of adjusted EBITDA margins near 22% on $900 million of revenue implies approximately $198 million of forward adjusted EBITDA, putting the forward EV/EBITDA at roughly 17.4 times on today's enterprise value. That figure is the appropriate starting point for a peer comparison.

AMETEK trades at 22.4 times trailing adjusted EBITDA with a ROIC of 12.5% and adjusted operating margins in the low-to-mid 20s. ITT trades at 18.1 times EBITDA with ROIC of 11.7% and adjusted operating margins near 22%. Curtiss-Wright, which operates across aerospace, defense, and nuclear power with adjusted operating margins near 19% and ROIC above 15%, trades at 33 times EBITDA and nearly 48 times FCF, the clearest illustration that the market is perfectly willing to pay substantial premiums for businesses with long-cycle defense and grid exposure when the revenue mix is legible. All three of these businesses are described by similar language: custom-engineered components, specification-driven customer relationships, long replacement cycles, and pricing power derived from technical differentiation rather than commodity volume.

Standex's Electronics segment fits that description precisely. The adjusted operating margin of 28.8% in the most recent quarter is not only above every peer shown above, it is approaching the margins that premium defense electronics businesses command. The difference is that Standex's company-level multiple is still shaped by the four smaller segments sitting alongside Electronics in the portfolio. That is both the explanation for the valuation gap and the source of the opportunity.

A simple sum-of-parts calculation makes the implied discount explicit. Non-Electronics segments contribute less than 40% of adjusted operating income across roughly half of company revenue, a profile consistent with 12 to 15 times EBITDA for businesses operating in mature, cyclical industrial end markets. Applying that range to the non-Electronics earnings base implies an enterprise value for those four segments of approximately $850 million to $1 billion. The remaining $2.4 to $2.6 billion represents the market's implied valuation of Electronics. Against Electronics adjusted EBITDA of approximately $120 million on a trailing basis, that implies a multiple of 20 to 22 times, below AMETEK's 22 times on lower margins and slower organic growth, and well below the premium that Curtiss-Wright's defense and grid-facing segments command. A business that earns more than its closest analog and grows faster than it has no structural reason to trade below it. That gap is the arithmetic of the opportunity.

The discount has three identifiable sources, each resolving on its own timeline. Portfolio complexity depresses the blended multiple: as long as four lower-margin segments sit alongside Electronics in the consolidated financials, the company-level EV/EBITDA trails the multiple that Electronics alone would command in isolation. That gap narrows organically as Electronics grows faster than the rest of the portfolio and closes further with each divestiture in non-core segments. Leverage of 2.3 times adjusted EBITDA, carried from the largest acquisition in the company's history, suppresses the multiple by a further turn or two relative to where a normalized balance sheet would trade; each quarter of debt repayment closes that gap. And Amran and Narayan, closed eighteen months ago, have not yet generated the earnings track record that institutional capital typically requires before assigning a structural re-rating. Six consecutive quarters of book-to-bill at or above 1.0 in Electronics is the beginning of that record. It is not the end.

The return from today's price resolves into three scenarios. In the base case, fiscal 2026 EBITDA of approximately $198 million against a flat 17 to 18 times forward multiple produces an enterprise value of $3.4 to $3.6 billion and, after net debt of $437.7 million, an implied equity value near $245 to $265 per share, roughly in line with today. That is the return of an owner who earns only earnings growth with no re-rating. In the upside case, a migration toward AMETEK's 22 times forward multiple on the same earnings base produces an enterprise value near $4.4 billion and equity value near $330 per share, a 32% return from today. That scenario does not require the market to become generous; it requires the market to value this segment's economics the way it already values AMETEK's, on lower margins and slower organic growth. In the downside case, if Grid demand disappoints materially and EBITDA falls to $170 million, a 15 times multiple produces an equity value near $180 per share, a 28% decline. That outcome requires both a demand shortfall and simultaneous multiple compression, a specific and identifiable scenario rather than a background risk.

The honest framing is that the base case preserves capital while earning modest earnings growth, the upside requires only recognition of economics the market already prices elsewhere, and the downside requires a specific negative confluence. The question this investment asks its owner is whether the Grid demand and new product contribution visible in the last six quarters is durable or front-loaded. The evidence, a book-to-bill above 1.0 for six consecutive quarters, capacity expansion funded by contracted commitments, and new product sales growing at 13% year-on-year, points toward durable. The next quarterly earnings, expected in early May, will deliver the first test of whether the third fiscal quarter continues that trajectory. Electronics organic growth, segment operating margin, and order flow are the three figures worth watching.

Final Thought

Standex International spent years building toward something. The portfolio pruning, the R&D investment, the new product cadence, the decision to write the largest check in the company's history for two Indian and American instrument transformer businesses, none of that was accidental. What the company looks like today, a business where the Electronics segment generates adjusted operating margins above 28% on revenue that is 52% of the total and growing at double-digit organic rates, is the intended destination of a multi-year capital allocation strategy that has been executed with unusual discipline.

Markets are slow to reprice transformations that happen inside historically unremarkable businesses. Standex spent long enough as a diversified industrial manufacturer that the label has proven sticky. The multiple still reflects that label more than it reflects the filings. For a long-term owner, that gap is the argument.

The electrical grid will require substantially more instrumentation, protection, and control infrastructure over the next decade than it has in any comparable period in modern history. The data centers driving AI infrastructure require cooling, power conditioning, and protection components that Standex now makes. Defense electronics demand is structurally elevated across every geography that matters to the company. These are not near-term catalysts. They are the demand conditions under which Standex's Grid and sensors businesses will continue to compound, quietly, in applications that rarely make headlines, at margins that most industrial businesses can only observe from a distance.

data.title: Wounded Secret Service Agent Was Shot in Chest, White House Says — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat

The Secret Service agent wounded in the shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner was hit in the chest, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Sunday. The agent "immediately moved to neutralize the shooter," she wrote on social media. The Secret Service said earlier that the officer who was shot was wearing a bullet-proof vest and has since been released from the hospital.

Leavitt added that the shooter "sought to assassinate the President and kill as many top Trump administration officials as possible."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Italy to extradite suspected Chinese hacker wanted by US authorities, says source
data.description:
The Italian government has decided to extradite a Chinese man wanted by U.S. authorities on hacking charges that include stealing COVID-19 medical research, a person with direct knowledge of the matter told Reuters.

The government's decision, which was first reported by Bloomberg, follows a ruling by an Italian court earlier this month which said Xu Zewei could be extradited.

A representative for Italy's government declined to comment. Xu's lawyer Enrico Giarda told Reuters that his client had not received any communications on the matter so far.

Xu was arrested in Milan on July 3 at the request of U.S. authorities, who have accused him of wire fraud and aggravated identity theft for his alleged role in computer piracy acts that took place between February 2020 and June 2021.

Following his arrest, Xu's lawyer said his client had been a victim of mistaken identity.

The U.S. Justice Department (DOJ) alleges that Xu has been hacking and stealing crucial COVID-19 research at the behest of the Chinese government.

The DOJ has said that Xu was part of a team of cyber experts who in 2020 targeted U.S.-based universities, immunologists, and virologists conducting research into COVID‑19 vaccines, treatment, and testing.

The DOJ also alleges that in 2021, Xu was part of a cyber-espionage group known as Hafnium, which infiltrated thousands of computers worldwide, including in the U.S.

data.title: British Ambassador Says King's Visit is Still On — WSJ
data.description:
By Meridith McGraw

Christian Turner, the British ambassador to the U.S., said during a brunch on Sunday-one day after the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting-that King Charles' upcoming visit to the U.S. would continue as scheduled.

"It's very British: Keep calm and carry on," Turner said at a brunch hosted by CNN. King Charles is set to arrive in Washington on Monday for a four-day visit that includes meetings with President Trump, a state dinner and an address to Congress.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Shooter's Written Statemet Discusses Security Breaches — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews and Josh Dawsey

The statement from the suspected shooter at the Washington Hilton, viewed by The Wall Street Journal, outlines security breaches at the hotel that made getting firearms into the building easier than he expected.

Cole Allen, identified by authorities as the alleged gunman Saturday night, wrote of his experience traveling to Washington and getting into the hotel. "Like, I expected security cameras at every bend, bugged hotel rooms, armed agents every 10 feet, metal detectors out the wazoo," he wrote. "What I got (who knows, maybe they're pranking me!) is nothing." It is not clear when he wrote down the remarks.

Guests of events at the hotel were generally asked to show just a print-out of a ticket, and magnetometers were not used until just before entering the ballroom where the dinner was held.

Allen wrote that he took the lax security, which guests also noticed, as a "sense of arrogance." "I walk in with multiple weapons and not a single person there considers the possibility that I could be a threat," he wrote.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Suspected Shooter Describes Himself as 'Friendly Federal Assassin' in Writings — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat and Josh Dawsey

The suspected shooter in Washington, D.C., called himself a "Friendly Federal Assassin" in writings authorities have tied to him. "I experience rage thinking about everything this administration has done," he wrote in a thousand-word document viewed by The Journal.

The suspect, Cole Allen, allegedly wrote that "doing something like this" felt awful. "I want to throw up," the document said. The author also expressed appreciation for people he said he likely wouldn't be able to talk to again, including his family, friends, students, colleagues and people he knew from church.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: DigiByte - DigiPay SDK Upgrade - 26 April 2026
data.description:
The DigiPay SDK upgrade for DigiByte (DGB) introduces easier payment links, sample applications, and idempotent payment handling, according to the DigiByte team’s post on X (source). Mechanically, this reduces integration friction for both non-developers and developers, and improves reliability for commerce workflows that must avoid duplicate charges or failed states. Greater ease of use for merchants and apps can support higher on-chain transaction volumes and more real-world payment use. If the SDK gains adoption, it can increase baseline demand for DGB as a medium of exchange, which tends to improve network value and may tighten exchange liquidity over time.

Dennis
@DennisOragon

DigiPay just got a lot easier to start using

payment links for non-devs. sample apps for devs. idempotency, so nothing breaks.

Here's everything that shipped https://t.co/fZ3qCaeLpR#DigiByte #DGB #DigiPay #CryptoPayments #BuildInPublic

Apr 26, 2026

data.title: Dego Finance - Binance Delisting - 28 April 2026
data.description:
Binance will remove spot trading support for Dego Finance (DEGO) and several other coins at 03:00 UTC on 28 April 2026, as indicated by the Binance notice on X about upcoming changes to trading pairs and related services (source). Delistings typically cut off a major liquidity venue, forcing holders who mainly use Binance to either sell beforehand or withdraw to other exchanges or self-custody. This often leads to wider spreads, lower depth, and potential forced selling pressure around the effective time, with longer term effects depending on how quickly other venues can absorb the displaced volume.

Binance
@binance

Notice on New Trading Pairs & Trading Bots Services on Binance Spot.

https://t.co/6QGrr3YlJb

Apr 26, 2026

data.title: Railgun - Ledger Talk - 30 April 2026
data.description:
Railgun (RAIL) researchers will host an X Spaces session on 30 April 2026 at 7:30 PM EST to discuss integration with Ledger hardware wallets, as outlined in the project’s announcement on X (source). The mechanism at stake is not the Spaces itself, but the planned hardware wallet support, which would allow privacy-focused users to secure RAIL assets through a mainstream custody device. This lowers self-custody friction and can broaden the addressable user base. Traders should watch the discussion for concrete timelines, supported chains, and any limitations, as clear implementation details can shift market confidence and affect positioning.

RAILGUN - Private Ethereum DeFi
@RAILGUN_Project

Join RAILGUN Researchers in a special Spaces discussing the Ledger hardware wallet integration at 7:30 PM EST 30th April.

Featuring: @tsu_kareta, @simonmasson2, @RenaudDUBOIS10, and @zknoxHQ. Set your reminder here: https://t.co/ExJC84rNdQ pic.twitter.com/mYAStJ8IcZ

Apr 26, 2026

data.title: Correspondents' Association Board to Discuss Next Steps — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews

Weijia Jiang, the president of the White House Correspondents' Association, issued a statement on Sunday morning, calling the shooting "a harrowing moment for everyone in attendance" at Saturday's dinner and expressing gratitude for U.S. Secret Service and all law-enforcement personnel. "We wish a full and speedy recovery to the officer who was injured in the line of duty," she wrote.

Trump has said that he wants to reschedule the dinner quickly. In her statement Jiang said that the association's board will meet to "assess what happened and determine how to proceed. We will provide updates as soon as any are available."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Bitcoin Leverage Builds as Price Stalls Below $80,000
data.description:
Bitcoin traders are stacking the long side of futures by more than three to one, according to Coinglass. The skew points to bullish conviction near $77,500 but raises the threat of forced selling on a sharp pullback.

The lopsided positioning led to open interest in BTC perpetuals sliding roughly 6% to 744,300 BTC over 24 hours. Traders are starting to trim leverage, but long bias still holds across major venues.

Long Bias Meets a Stalling Spot Price

Bitcoin failed to clear $80,000 earlier this week and has since drifted toward $77,500, according to Yahoo Finance. That stall has done little to shake long-side conviction. The long/short ratio on Coinglass still shows more than 3 longs per short.

History shows that extreme imbalances often precede contrarian moves. Crowded one-sided trades become easy fuel for short-term reversals.

Coinglass logged $22.44 million in long liquidations on April 25 against $11.60 million on the short side. The roughly two-to-one wipeout hints bulls are absorbing more pain even as account-level positioning stays heavily long.

Bitcoin Liquidation Map Flags Concentrated Risk Pockets

The Coinglass map shows dense clusters of leveraged long positions stacked beneath the current spot price. The arrangement historically amplifies downside moves through cascading liquidations.

Each liquidated long adds market sell flow that can push the price into the next cluster.

Earlier in April, $71 million in long positions sat at risk under $77,300. Above $78,000, short-squeeze conditions fueled a sweep that wiped out millions in bearish bets. Rising leverage and open interest have repeatedly preceded sharp corrections this cycle.

Whether spot defends $77,000 may decide if the next move is a controlled cool-off or a sharper liquidation cascade. For now, the imbalance leaves the market structurally fragile despite the bullish optics.

data.title: Manifesto Linked to Suspected Shooter Says He Targeted Trump, Administration Officials — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews and Josh Dawsey

A manifesto authorities said they have tied to Cole Allen, the suspect in the correspondents' dinner shooting, outlines why Allen allegedly traveled from California to Washington and details plans for a mass shooting, while mainly targeting the president because "I am no longer willing to permit a pedophile, rapist, and traitor to coat my hands with his crimes."

The thousand-word document reviewed by The Wall Street Journal said that Allen wanted to target administration officials "prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest" and that "I would still go through most everyone here to get to the targets if it were absolutely necessary .... but I really hope it doesn't come to that."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Cheffelo announces leadership transition as Chief Business Development Officer Klaus Toft Nørgaard steps down
data.description:
After more than 20 years with the company, including founding Cheffelo’s Danish business, Klaus Toft Nørgaard will, by mutual agreement, step down from his role as Chief Business Development Officer at the end of October to focus on other business ventures. During the coming period, Klaus will continue to lead Cheffelo’s pilot launch in Finland as well as lead the ongoing strategy development.

“When Klaus founded RetNemt in 2006, it was one of the first companies globally to offer mealkits, pioneering a new category and a new way to help families solve dinner,” says Walker Kinman, CEO of Cheffelo. “Since RetNemt became part of Cheffelo in 2016, Klaus has continued to play an important role in our growth, while also being a highly valued and respected colleague. As he now chooses to focus on other business ventures, my colleagues and I want to thank him for his longstanding contributions and wish him all the best in the future.”

“Looking back to when we started RetNemt and seeing that we are today a profitable, growing Scandinavian mealkit company is something I am very proud to be part of,” says Klaus Toft Nørgaard, Chief Business Development Officer at Cheffelo. “Families are still balancing work, commitments and hobbies, while wanting to put good food on the table, so I sincerely believe in a strong future for mealkits. I am excited to continue working with the team on our upcoming launch in Finland, as well as on strategy development, and after October I look forward to focusing on other business ventures.”

Klaus’ responsibilities will be redistributed within the Cheffelo management team, in line with the company’s growth strategy and there are no plans to immediately recruit a replacement for this role.

For further information, please contact:

Peter Bodor, Head of Corporate Communications, Cheffelo

Telephone (SWE): +46 706 48 70 65

E-mail: peter.bodor@cheffelo.com

Erik Bergman, CFO, Cheffelo

Telephone (SWE): +46 707 74 49 73

E-mail: erik.bergman@cheffelo.com

About Cheffelo

Cheffelo is a leading, profitable Scandinavian mealkit provider that helps people eat well without the hassle of planning and shopping. Since 2006, Cheffelo has made it easier to enjoy varied, nutritious homecooked meals by delivering personalized meal kits with minimal food waste. With nearly 400 highly engaged employees, the company manages its own production facilities, integrating customer-unique packing processes and proprietary technology infrastructure to streamline operations and enable epic customer experiences. The company operates under the brands Linas in Sweden, Godtlevert in Norway, and RetNemt in Denmark. In 2025, Cheffelo generated SEK 1.2 billion in revenue and delivered approximately 17 million meals. Cheffelo is listed on Nasdaq First North Premier Growth Market (ticker: CHEF).

www.cheffelo.com

www.linkedin.com/company/cheffelo

Attachments

Cheffelo announces leadership transition as Chief Business Development Officer Klaus Toft Nørgaard steps down

data.title: Brent Oil Set to Rise Again
data.description:
Brent crude is set to extend gains when markets reopen after the weekend, as hopes of reopening the Strait of Hormuz faded once more.

Iran’s foreign minister left Pakistan on Sunday without meeting US officials, following President Trump’s order to halt negotiations.

Last week, brent crude rose 17% as supply concerns remained elevated as the key shipping route remains largely closed.

The ongoing US naval blockade continues to restrict Iranian crude exports and remains a major obstacle to progress.

Analysts say that even if the strait reopens, oil flows could take months to normalize, keeping supplies tight and pressure on global energy markets.

data.title: Security Stepped Up at Post-Dinner Brunch — WSJ
data.description:
By Josh Dawsey

Organizers of a well-known post-White House Correspondents' Dinner brunch stepped up security in the aftermath of last night's shooting. Attendees passed through magnetometers before entering the event.

The brunch, hosted by billionaire Robert Allbritton, includes members of the media, politicians and other high-profile figures.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Crude Oil Set to Extend Gains
data.description:
Crude oil is set to rise again when markets reopen after the weekend, after easing below $95 a barrel on Friday as hopes of reopening the Strait of Hormuz faded once more.

Iran’s foreign minister left Pakistan on Sunday without meeting US officials, following President Trump’s order to halt negotiations.

WTI surged 13% last week, its biggest weekly gain since early March, as the key shipping route remains largely closed.

The ongoing U.S. naval blockade continues to restrict Iranian crude exports and remains a major obstacle to progress.

Analysts say that even if the strait reopens, oil flows could take months to normalize, keeping supplies tight and pressure on global energy markets.

data.title: Tillis Drops Objection to Warsh's Fed Nomination — WSJ
data.description:
By Nick Timiraos and Lindsay Wise

Sen. Thom Tillis (R., N.C.) said Sunday he would support Kevin Warsh's confirmation as Federal Reserve chair, clearing the last significant obstacle to installing President Trump's pick to succeed Jerome Powell.

Tillis had withheld his vote for months, saying he wouldn't advance any Fed nominee while the Justice Department's criminal investigation of Powell remained open. The probe appeared to end on Friday, when U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro said her office was closing its inquiry into Powell's testimony to Congress about the Fed's building renovations and referring the matter to the Fed's inspector general. The inspector general, who reports to Powell, had already been conducting an audit at Powell's request since July.

In a statement Sunday, Tillis said he took the Justice Department "at its word" that the investigation is closed and that any appeal of a federal judge's March ruling against the probe's grand jury subpoenas "will be with respect to legal principles and not for the purpose of reissuing subpoenas." He said that only a criminal referral from the inspector general would justify reopening the case. "With these assurances, I look forward to supporting Kevin Warsh's confirmation," Tillis said.

The probe's status had remained ambiguous in the hours after Pirro's Friday announcement. Trump told reporters Saturday that the case was "not dropped." But Tillis received assurances from the Justice Department on the conditions for reopening, which he laid out in his public statement Sunday, according to people familiar with the matter.

Tillis explained his reasoning in an interview that aired Sunday morning on NBC's "Meet the Press." He said that the Justice Department had represented to him that the probe is "completely and fully ended" and that only a criminal referral from the Fed's inspector general could revive the case.

Any appeal by Pirro of last month's ruling against the subpoenas, he added, "would only be used to potentially abrogate or change the ruling for administrative purposes, not as a basis for reopening the investigation."

Tillis said his concern throughout had been that prosecutors were using the criminal probe as a "lever" to pressure Powell to leave early.

Tillis's statement clears the path to confirm Warsh by May 15, when Powell's term as chair expires. Republicans hold a 13-11 majority on the Senate Banking Committee; Tillis's prior opposition would have stalled the nomination there. The committee has scheduled a vote on Warsh's nomination for Wednesday, which would clear the path for a Senate vote by the week of May 11. The Senate isn't scheduled to be in session for the first week of May.

Write to Nick Timiraos at Nick.Timiraos@wsj.com and Lindsay Wise at lindsay.wise@wsj.com

data.title: Mentalist Guessed Karoline Leavitt's Baby Name Seconds Before Shooting — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews

Mentalist Oz Pearlman, who was supposed to be the entertainment for the correspondents dinner, told ABC's Jonathan Karl that seconds before shots were fired outside of the ballroom, he was guessing the name of White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt's baby, scheduled to arrive next week.

"I was guessing letter by letter, how many letters were in the name, and then right at the moment when you see it happen, I wrote down the name and said, 'How did I do,' and then I turn around," Pearlman said. At that moment, he said the first lady gasped and loud noises were heard outside the ballroom, and everyone dived under the table. Pearlman said Leavitt has given him permission to share that she plans to name her baby Vivian.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Shooter Bought Guns Legally — WSJ
data.description:
By Sadie Gurman and Josh Dawsey

The suspected shooter legally purchased the shotgun and handgun used in Saturday night's attack outside the White House Correspondent's Dinner from a store in California, people briefed on the FBI's investigation said. Investigators plan to head to the store where he bought them in Los Angeles to conduct interviews, the people said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: FBI Trying to Examine Suspected Gunman's Cell Phone — WSJ
data.description:
By Sadie Gurman and Josh Dawsey

Investigators are still trying to examine the contents of the suspected gunman's cell phone, which officials said he was carrying when he opened fire outside the White House Correspondent's Dinner. A person briefed on the probe said investigators hope to be able to access the phone in coming days.

Investigators are also examining a laptop and a hard drive found in the suspect's hotel room. People familiar with the investigation said authorities also found in the room a bookbag, a loaded 10-round magazine, two knives, a Metro receipt and a filtered mask.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Oruka Therapeutics to Host Conference Call to Report Week 16 Data for ORKA-001 from the Ongoing EVERLAST-A Trial on April 27, 2026
data.description:
MENLO PARK, Calif., April 26, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Oruka Therapeutics, Inc. (“Oruka”) , a clinical-stage biotechnology company developing novel biologics designed to set a new standard for the treatment of chronic skin diseases, today announced it will report Week 16 data for ORKA-001 from the ongoing EVERLAST-A Phase 2a trial in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis on Monday, April 27, 2026. Following the announcement, the company will host a conference call and webcast at 8:00 a.m. ET to discuss the results.

Webcast Details

Oruka Therapeutics’ live webcast of the EVERLAST-A results will begin on Monday, April 27th, at 8:00 a.m. ET. The live webcast can be accessed via this link, or through the Investors section on the company’s website at https://ir.orukatx.com/news-events/events-presentations. A replay of the webcast will be available following the call.

About Oruka Therapeutics

Oruka Therapeutics is developing novel biologics designed to set a new standard for the treatment of chronic skin diseases. Oruka’s mission is to offer patients suffering from chronic skin diseases like plaque psoriasis the greatest possible freedom from their condition by achieving high rates of complete disease clearance with dosing as infrequently as once or twice a year. Oruka is advancing a proprietary portfolio of potentially best-in-class antibodies that were engineered by Paragon Therapeutics and target the core mechanisms underlying plaque psoriasis and other dermatologic and inflammatory diseases. For more information, visit www.orukatx.com and follow Oruka on LinkedIn.

Investor Contact:

Alan Lada

(650)-606-7911

alan.lada@orukatx.com

data.title: BONE Network Heats Up: 93K Holders After Massive Weekly Surge
data.description:
Wallets holding at least 1 million BONE quietly grew their positions by over 4% in April — a detail that might say more about where this token is headed than any single-week spike in new addresses.

Validator Activity Drives Holder Surge

BONE, the gas token powering Shiba Inu’s Layer-2 blockchain Shibarium, crossed 93,000 holder addresses this week after adding 5,653 new wallets in seven days. That weekly growth rate hit 87%, a sharp jump from the prior week.

According to the Shibizens X account, operated by the Shibarium team, most of that growth traces back to validator re-delegations on the network rather than a wave of new retail buyers. Etherscan data confirmed the total at 93,010 at the time of reporting.

Shibarium | SHIB.IO
@Shibizens

$BONE holder count has surpassed 93,000 addresses.

+5,653 new holders in the last 7 days → ~87% increase vs the previous week

Key drivers: • Validator re-delegations on Shibarium

Observed trends: • Tokens moving off exchanges into non-custodial wallets • Increased… pic.twitter.com/svq50JlfYP

Apr 23, 2026

On-chain data also shows BONE tokens moving away from centralized exchanges and into non-custodial wallets. Reports from Shibizens indicate transaction activity is climbing alongside an expanding active user base — trends that typically point to reduced short-term selling pressure and growing user confidence in the network.

Big Wallets Holding Long, Holding More

The 4.2% position increase among large holders in April has pushed their collective share to almost 60% of total supply. These top wallets aren’t new to the token. Based on reports from Shibizens, their average holding period sits around 412 days — well over a year — suggesting major participants are focused on Shibarium’s longer-term trajectory rather than reacting to short-term price swings.

Trading volume also moved sharply higher, rising 51.77% over a 24-hour window to reach $1.7 million. That spike in volume came even as BONE’s price remained under pressure, trading around $0.05766 — a 2.5% drop in a single day. Price Gap Remains A Heavy Overhang

The numbers paint a complicated picture. Year-to-date, BONE has lost 28% of its value. Over the past month alone, it shed more than 10%. Those losses are part of a much larger decline that has stretched over years.

BONE once traded at $41.67 — its all-time high, reached in September 2021. At its current price, the token sits 99.86% below that peak. The gap between where BONE trades today and where it once stood is something no single week of holder growth can paper over.

The BONE network surpassed 93,000 holders following what the Shibarium team is calling a massive weekly surge. Whether the holder growth, rising volume, and accumulation by large wallets point to something bigger — or simply reflect routine network activity — remains to be seen.

Featured image from Unsplash, chart from TradingView

data.title: The Pentagon Needed Rare Earths — and Found a Supplier in Malaysia — WSJ
data.description:
By Jon Emont | Photography by Suzanne Lee for WSJ

KUANTAN, Malaysia — The Pentagon's push to get its hands on the rarest of the rare-earth elements leads all the way to this small port city in Malaysia.

Here, Lynas Rare Earths, an Australian company, has begun pumping out heavy rare earths, the elusive kind that China dominates.

"No one had made a separated heavy rare earth outside of China in 20 years," said Amanda Lacaze, Lynas's chief executive. The company's chief operating officer, Pol Le Roux, said it had actually been 30 years.

When China cut off exports of heavy rare-earth elements during trade tensions last year, automobile factories in the U.S. and Europe were forced to stop production. Now, Lynas is at the vanguard of an effort by the U.S. and allies to prevent Beijing from using its monopoly power to squeeze the rest of the world.

The Pentagon is opening its wallet in unusual ways to ensure supplies. In March 2026, Lynas announced a preliminary $96 million deal in which the Pentagon would purchase Lynas's rare earths.

And Las Vegas-headquartered MP Materials, backed by billions of dollars in U.S. government support, is planning its own refinery for heavy rare earths that is set to come online later this year.

Last month, Lynas began producing samarium oxide, a difficult-to-source rare earth in high military demand that is used in heat-resistant magnets for jet fighters and missiles.

"There is no doubt that 2025 was the wake-up call the United States needed to undertake bold industrial policy," said Gracelin Baskaran, who leads the critical minerals program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies in Washington.

Rare-earth minerals are already mined outside of China, including Lynas's, which come from Western Australia. To gain independence from Chinese supplies, the hard part is building refining capacity. It often requires hundreds of stages to separate the rare earths using industrial acids.

For more than a decade, Lynas has had a refinery here in Kuantan, a Malaysian chemical-industry center. But it only produced light rare earths, which tend to be more common, while it sold heavy rare earths to China for processing. Last year, as the U.S.-China trade war was at its peak, Lynas finished a new heavy rare-earths processor in Kuantan.

Machinery whirs loudly as a rare-earth mixture is bathed in hydrochloric acid and gradually separated into pure oxides that can be shipped to customers. Terbium, used in powerful magnets, comes out a deep, rich brown. Dysprosium appears as a whitish powder.

Because of their small quantities, the heavy rare earths are fitted into knee-high 55-pound cans that could be worth tens of thousands of dollars, while less-valuable rare earths such as cerium are stuffed into 1800 pound sacks.

Heavy rare-earth elements are sprinkled in magnets so they can function at higher temperatures. That is important in cars and planes whose engines run hot.

Lynas and MP Materials are two of the leading Western rare-earths producers, and Washington wants more suppliers. In February, the U.S. International Development Finance Corp. extended $565 million in loans to Serra Verde, which operates a mine in Brazil with significant reserves of heavy rare earths.

Last week USA Rare Earth, a Stillwater, Okla., company that has recently commissioned equipment to make rare-earth magnets, said it would acquire Serra Verde in a deal valued at about $2.8 billion, part of an arrangement that will ensure a steady supply of heavy rare earths to the U.S.

Not everything has gone smoothly with U.S. efforts. Lynas has said there is "significant uncertainty" on whether it will go ahead with an effort to build a rare earth processing facility in Texas, which was allocated $258 million in Pentagon grant funding in 2023. The estimated project costs ballooned because of challenges in handling wastewater.

Instead, Lynas is building out a second, larger heavy rare-earth processing facility in Kuantan, expected to be completed in 2028.

Lynas scored a quick win when it achieved commercial production of samarium last month. The mineral had been refined almost exclusively in China, causing a scramble among defense suppliers last year when China cut off exports in April. A report from the U.S. Geological Survey last year found samarium was the highest-risk mineral for disruption, with shortages potentially costing U.S. industry billions of dollars.

American defense companies face a 2027 government deadline to ensure that no rare earths in their supply chain for magnets come from China. Lacaze said Lynas was supplying its non-Chinese rare earths to Japanese magnet makers that in turn supply the U.S. defense industry.

Still, Lacaze expressed concern that Western nations weren't doing enough to ensure adequate demand. Military demand for rare earths is relatively small, so she advocated tax credits to induce larger commercial buyers — such as makers of cars and electronics — to choose non-Chinese rare-earth magnets.

Baskaran, the critical-minerals specialist, said the effort to achieve rare-earth independence was still in its early stages. "While momentum is real, translating these announcements into production takes years," she said.

Write to Jon Emont at jonathan.emont@wsj.com

data.title: Sen. Thom Tillis Clears Kevin Warsh's Path to Fed Chair — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nicole Goodkind

The last major obstacle to Kevin Warsh's confirmation as Federal Reserve chair has seemingly been removed, putting President Donald Trump's nominee on a tight track to take over the central bank before current chair Jerome Powell's term expires May 15.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) announced Sunday on NBC's Meet the Press that he will support Warsh's confirmation, ending the standoff that had threatened to stall the nomination in the Senate Banking Committee. The committee is scheduled to vote Wednesday on advancing Warsh to a full Senate floor vote.

Tillis had made his position clear from the start: he wouldn't let Warsh out of committee as long as the Justice Department's criminal investigation of Powell remained active. With Republicans holding a 12-10 edge on the Banking Committee, one defection was enough to block the nomination.

The logjam broke Friday, when U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro announced her office was dropping the criminal probe and handing the matter to the Fed's own inspector general, which has been investigating cost overruns from the central bank's multibillion-dollar Washington headquarters renovation since last July.

Pirro had previously vowed to appeal a federal judge's twice-issued ruling blocking her subpoenas to the Fed, a stance that had kept Tillis unwavering through Warsh's confirmation hearing last week.

Tillis said Sunday he received assurances from the Justice Department that the criminal investigation wouldn't be reopened unless the inspector general issues a criminal referral.

"I take the Department of Justice at its word," he said in a statement posted on social media, adding that he now looks forward to supporting Warsh's confirmation. "He is an outstanding nominee, and it is time for the Federal Reserve to move beyond this distraction and return its full attention to its mission."

The timing remains tight. The Senate is in recess the week of May 4, and will likely hold a floor vote upon its return, which leaves a narrow window before Powell's term ends. If confirmation doesn't come in time, Powell has said he would remain as chair pro tempore, a scenario Trump has already signaled he won't accept, telling Fox Business he would attempt to fire Powell if he doesn't leave when his chairmanship expires.

Warsh, if confirmed, will arrive at the Fed with an ambitious agenda — to both lower interest rates and create a smaller balance sheet.

The Federal Open Market Committee he'll inherit is tilted the other way, with inflation still running near 3% and oil prices elevated since the start of war with Iran in February.

Write to Nicole Goodkind at nicole.goodkind@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trump Says King Charles Is Still Heading to Washington in Coming Days — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat

President Trump said he expected King Charles to move forward with his planned visit to Washington, D.C. on Monday. "He's coming and we're going to have a great time," the president said on Fox News Sunday.

Buckingham Palace said the king "is being kept fully informed of developments" and was relieved to hear no one was harmed. "As you would expect, a number of discussions will be taking place throughout the day to discuss with U.S. colleagues and our respective teams to what degree the events of Saturday evening may or may not impact on the operational planning for the visit," a palace spokesperson said. The king was scheduled to meet with Trump this week to coincide with the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Ethereum Foundation Unstakes Nearly $50 Million ETH While ETF Inflow Streak
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ethereum Foundation began unstaking nearly $49 million in ETH, with Arkham flagging the move as it nears its 70,000 ETH staking target.
  • The unstaking process involves converting wstETH back to liquid ETH via Lido once withdrawals are complete.
  • Ethereum trades near $2,300 as analysts remain split, citing geopolitical uncertainty and key technical levels between $2,150 and $2,500.

The Ethereum Foundation began unstaking nearly $49 million in Ethereum (ETH), drawing attention as the funds approached liquidity.

On Sunday, Arkham Intelligence flagged that the Ethereum Foundation had begun unwinding part of its staking position shortly after it had nearly reached its stated goal of 70,000 staked ETH, signaling a shift in how it managed its on-chain holdings.

According to Arkham, the Foundation deposited wrapped staked Ethereum (wstETH) into Lido’s unstaking contract, a process that would convert the assets back into ETH once withdrawals are completed.

Market Participants Remain Divided 

The move comes at a time when Ethereum’s price was trading at $2,334, up by 0.7% over the past 24 hours. On Stocktwits, the retail sentiment around ETH remained in an ‘extremely bearish’ zone, while chatter stayed ‘normal’ over the past day.

Broader geopolitical developments are also feeding into sentiment. Analyst Ted Pillows noted that “ETH is going sideways for now,” adding that “US-Iran peace talks have been canceled, which means next week will be very crucial.” He said a move above $2,400 could push Ethereum toward “$2,470 to $2,500 liquidity,” while a breakdown below $2,300 may lead to “a retest of $2,150 to $2,200 support level.”

At the same time, technical patterns were contributing to the cautious outlook. CGT Trader, another crypto analyst, said that “price just printed a head and shoulders pattern on ETH with a descending neckline, a sign of particular weakness,” though he noted the setup was not yet confirmed. He added that “if this plays out, it could bring [the] price back down to $2000.”

Meanwhile, US spot Ethereum ETFs recorded a 10-day streak of net inflows last week, according to data from SoSo Value, pointing to sustained institutional demand.

data.title: Raizen proposes alternative terms to creditors for debt restructuring, Bloomberg reports
data.description:
Brazilian fuel and sugar company Raizen sent an alternative proposal to creditors as it tries to iron out terms of a 65 billion-real ($13.05 billion) debt restructuring, Bloomberg News reported on Sunday.

The company told creditors it is in talks to raise up to 5 billion reais in fresh capital, the report added, citing people familiar with the matter.

The proposed capital raise would be in addition to 4 billion reais in funding already pledged by Shell and Rubens Ometto, Cosan's founder and controlling shareholder, Bloomberg reported, adding that the source of the additional funds was unclear.

Brazilian conglomerate Cosan, which jointly controls Raizen with Shell, is not injecting cash into the company, according to the report.

Raizen is resisting creditor demands that shareholders surrender a majority of board seats or that executives be held responsible for potential future liabilities, but has agreed to set up a creditors' committee to strengthen governance oversight, the report added.

Reuters could not immediately verify the report. Raizen and Cosan did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside of business hours. Shell declined to comment.

Raizen has struggled with managing its debts after a period of heavy capital spending, unfavorable weather and wildfires that hurt harvests and reduced cane-crushing volumes.

The company has less than two months to gain final approval for the proposed restructuring, but it is unclear how long current talks will take.

($1 = 4.9795 reais)

data.title: Trump Says Suspect Motivated By Hatred for Christians — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat

President Trump said the suspected shooter at the White House Correspondents' Dinner was a "very troubled guy" who "hates Christians." "The guy was a sick guy when you read his manifesto," he said on Fox News Sunday. "It was a religious thing. It was strongly anti-Christian." Trump also said the suspect "had a lot of hatred in his heart for quite a while."

"I think his sister or his brother actually was complaining about it," Trump said. "Even complaining to law enforcement." The president said the suspect's family knew he had difficulties. "Maybe they should have reported him a little more strongly, probably."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
